request.question: Which artist did Anthony Toby "Tony" Hiller appear with that liked showering himself (and others) with confetti?

title: Ichthyosis with confetti
Tony Hiller
KDrew
Residential water use in the U.S. and Canada
List of most disliked YouTube videos

sentences: Ichthyosis en confetti, also known as ichthyosis with confetti, congenital reticular ichthyosiform erythroderma (CRIE) and ichthyosis variegata, is a very rare form of congenital ichthyosis in which healthy patches of normal skin co-exist within the abnormal skin areas.

The condition is caused by a frameshift mutation in the keratin 10 gene (KRT10); mutant keratin 10 accumulates in the nucleolus, a sub-nuclear structure, rather than within cellular intermedite filaments like the wild-type protein.
Children with the condition exhibit red, flaky skin; however, for reasons not yet totally clear, wild type clonal patches of skin start to appear, in place of the red, flaky skin.
Due to the clonal nature of the growth of the normal skin cells, it appears the patient is covered with confetti, hence the name of the condition.
It has been hypothesized that this is the result of a combination of mitotic recombination and natural selection within the skin.
Anthony Toby "Tony" Hiller (born 30 July 1927) is a British songwriter.
He began his musical career as a member of the song and dance duo The Hiller Brothers, sharing the stage with his brother Irving.

The Hiller Brothers appeared with many artists of the time including Alma Cogan, Tommy Cooper, Val Doonican, Matt Monro, The Shadows, Bernard Manning, Kathy Kirby, Roger Whittaker, Rip Taylor, Gene Vincent, Lance Percival, Tessie O'Shea, Frank Ifield, Deep River Boys, The Dallas Boys, Clark Brothers, Paul Melba and Ray Burns.
Kevin Nicholas Drew, professionally and better known as KDrew, is an artist, producer and DJ from New Jersey.
KDrew produces primarily EDM and is perhaps best known for his remixes for Zedd and Lady Gaga, although his original records "Bullseye" and Circles have generated millions of plays online.
The Boston Celtics of the NBA currently use "Circles" as the intro music for their home games at TD Garden.
KDrew has also done official remixes for Nelly Furtado, Adventure Club, T.I. and others and collaborated with Taryn Manning on "Summer Ashes", which was released on July 23, 2013.
On February 5, 2015 MTV premiered KDrew's music video for "Let Me Go".
KDrew performed at Global Dance Festival at Red Rocks Amphitheatre.
He co-wrote the song "I Want You to Know", by Zedd featuring Selena Gomez, alongside Zedd and Ryan Tedder.
KDrew also features as an additional producer on the tracks "Done With Love" and "Straight Into the Fire" off Zedd's "True Colors" album.
KDrew co-produced two songs on Hilary Duff's Breathe In.
Breathe Out.
album, "Confetti" and "Arms Around a Memory".
Residential water use (also called domestic use, household use, or tap water use) includes all indoor and outdoor uses of drinking quality water at single-family and multifamily dwellings.

These uses include a number of defined purposes (or water end uses) such as flushing toilets, washing clothes and dishes, showering and bathing, drinking, food preparation, watering lawns and gardens, and maintaining swimming pools.
Some of these end uses are detectable (and measurable) while others are more difficult to gauge.
This list of most disliked YouTube videos contains the top 40 videos with the most dislikes of all time, as derived from YouTube charts.
The dislike count is taken directly from the page of the video itself.
YouTube implemented a like and dislike button on these pages in 2010, part of a major redesign of the site.

This served as a replacement for their five-star rating system; YouTube's designers found the previous system ineffective because the options to rate a video between two and four stars were rarely selected.
Of the 40 videos in this list, 14 also appear in the list of most viewed YouTube videos and 10 appear in the list of most liked YouTube videos.
Coleonema pulchellum, commonly known as confetti bush, buchu, diosma or breath of heaven, is a shrub which is endemic to South Africa.
It grows to between 0.8 and 1 metre high.
Single pink flowers to about 8 mm in diameter appear between May and October in the species native range.
Toby Love is the self-titled debut studio album by Puerto Rican-American performer Toby Love.
It was released on September 5, 2006 by Sony BMG Norte.
The album featured collaborations with Puerto Rican rapper Voltio and Puerto Rican duo R.K.M & Ken-Y, among others.

Upon release, the album debuted at number twenty-seven on the "Billboard" Latin Albums chart, number forty-two on the "Billboard" Heatseekers Albums chart, number three on the Mid-Atlantic area division of the "Billboard" Heatseekers Albums chart and number eleven on "Billboard" Latin Rhythm Albums chart.

The album spawned three singles, with the lead single, "Tengo Un Amor", becoming a success, in the Latin market, reaching number one in the "Billboard" Latin Rhythm Airplay chart, the top three of both the "Billboard" Latin Songs and "Billboard" Tropical Songs charts, while peaking at number 100 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 chart in the United States.
"Toby Love" was awarded a "Billboard" Latin Music Award for "R&B/Hip-Hop Album of the Year".
This list of most liked YouTube videos contains the top 40 videos with the most likes of all time, as derived from YouTube charts.
The like count is taken directly from the page of the video itself.
YouTube implemented a like and dislike button on these pages in March 2010, part of a major redesign of the site.

This served as a replacement for their five-star rating system; YouTube's designers found the previous system ineffective because the options to rate a video between two and four stars were rarely selected.
Of the 40 videos in this list, 37 also appear in the list of most viewed YouTube videos and 15 appear in the list of most disliked YouTube videos.
All videos in this list are music videos.

"There's No Place Like London" is a song co-written by Lynsey de Paul and Gerard Kenny, that was first recorded by Shirley Bassey with a 54 piece orchestra, produced by de Paul and released as a single in 1986.

She performed it in 1986 as part of her “Live from the Piccadilly” show as well as on the 1987 Royal Variety Performance and ironically for her "Shirley Bassey live" in Berlin in honour of 750 years of Berlin in 1987.
It was released for the first time on CD to tie-in with the UK held Olympic Games in 2012 on the album, "Olympic Tracks".
It is listed in the BBC music database which catalogues songs they play.
Speaking about the recording of the song in an interview, de Paul said "we wrote.
"There's No Place Like London", and the Tourist Board liked it, and then we thought of an artist and I thought of Shirley Bassey.
So I approached her and asked her to sing it and she really liked and that's the way round it came, it didn't come that I wrote for her I just had the song and said would you sing it.
And she was great."
It is still one of the most played Bassey songs on BBC radio.
Two different live Bassey performances of the song appear on the Odeon Entertainment 2010 DVD, "Shirley Bassey On TV".
Charles Elmer "Rip" Taylor, Jr. (born January 13, 1935) is an American actor and comedian.
He is known for his exuberance and flamboyant personality, including his wild moustache and his habit of showering himself (and others) with confetti.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

He began his musical career as a member of the song and dance duo The Hiller Brothers, sharing the stage with his brother Irving.  The Hiller Brothers appeared with many artists of the time including Alma Cogan, Tommy Cooper, Val Doonican, Matt Monro, The Shadows, Bernard Manning, Kathy Kirby, Roger Whittaker, Rip Taylor, Gene Vincent, Lance Percival, Tessie O'Shea, Frank Ifield, Deep River Boys, The Dallas Boys, Clark Brothers, Paul Melba and Ray Burns.

Rip Taylor | Charles Elmer "Rip" Taylor, Jr. (born January 13, 1935) is an American actor and comedian.  He is known for his exuberance and flamboyant personality, including his wild moustache and his habit of showering himself (and others) with confetti.

Ichthyosis with confetti | Ichthyosis en confetti, also known as ichthyosis with confetti, congenital reticular ichthyosiform erythroderma (CRIE) and ichthyosis variegata, is a very rare form of congenital ichthyosis in which healthy patches of normal skin co-exist within the abnormal skin areas.  The condition is caused by a frameshift mutation in the keratin 10 gene (KRT10); mutant keratin 10 accumulates in the nucleolus, a sub-nuclear structure, rather than within cellular intermedite filaments like the wild-type protein.  Children with the condition exhibit red, flaky skin; however, for reasons not yet totally clear, wild type clonal patches of skin start to appear, in place of the red, flaky skin.  Due to the clonal nature of the growth of the normal skin cells, it appears the patient is covered with confetti, hence the name of the condition.  It has been hypothesized that this is the result of a combination of mitotic recombination and natural selection within the skin.

Tony Booth (artist) | Anthony "Tony" Booth (22 June 1933 – 11 January 2017) was a British commercial artist best known as the original poster artist for The Beatles and other bands in the Merseybeat era.  He was known around Liverpool in the early 1960s as Brian Epstein's "right-hand man".

Scurfield | The family name Scurfield appears to be of Old Norse, or Viking origin.  “Scur” in Old English is “shower” as in rain.  In Old Norse “skur” also carries the meaning shower, as in shower of rain, or a shower of arrows.  The most likely origin of the surname Scurfield, therefore, is the name of an ancient field of battle.

Tony Reali | Anthony Joseph Paul "Tony" Reali (born July 4, 1978) is an American sports personality and television host of "Around the Horn" on ESPN.  In April 2014 it was announced he would be a contributor on social media for "Good Morning America", which he began on October 8, 2014 but has not appeared in 2015, other than a couple sports related segments introduced as "ESPN's Tony Reali".  He was "Stat Boy" on "Pardon the Interruption" from the show's debut in 2001 until late 2014.

The Careless Years | The Careless Years is a 1957 film from United Artists directed by Arthur Hiller and produced by Edward Lewis.  The film was the directorial debut for Hiller.  The film stars Dean Stockwell and Natalie Trundy in an early film appearance.
response.summary_1: Rip Taylor — Tony Hiller appeared with Rip Taylor; Rip Taylor is known for "showering himself (and others) with confetti." (passages 1–2)
response.hop2_query: Tony Hiller Rip Taylor appeared with
hop2_docs: Tony Hiller | Anthony Toby "Tony" Hiller (born 30 July 1927) is a British songwriter.  He began his musical career as a member of the song and dance duo The Hiller Brothers, sharing the stage with his brother Irving.  The Hiller Brothers appeared with many artists of the time including Alma Cogan, Tommy Cooper, Val Doonican, Matt Monro, The Shadows, Bernard Manning, Kathy Kirby, Roger Whittaker, Rip Taylor, Gene Vincent, Lance Percival, Tessie O'Shea, Frank Ifield, Deep River Boys, The Dallas Boys, Clark Brothers, Paul Melba and Ray Burns.

Tony Taylor (footballer, born 1946) | Anthony "Tony" Taylor (born 6 September 1946 in Glasgow) is a former professional footballer, who made 345 appearances in the Scottish League and Football League playing mainly as a left back.

Sunny Honey Girl | "Sunny Honey Girl" is a song by the British musical group The Pipkins.  The song is written by Cook, Greenaway, John Goodison and Tony Hiller.  The song appeared in The Pipkins' 1970 album "Gimme Dat Ding", a split album released jointly with The Sweet, with The Sweet on side 1 and The Pipkins, on side 2.

Rip Taylor | Charles Elmer "Rip" Taylor, Jr. (born January 13, 1935) is an American actor and comedian.  He is known for his exuberance and flamboyant personality, including his wild moustache and his habit of showering himself (and others) with confetti.

Over You (Anne Murray song) | "Over You" is a song written by Byron Hill and Tony Hiller and performed by Anne Murray.  The song reached number 11 on the Canadian Adult Contemporary chart and number 29 on the Canadian Country chart in 1995.  The song appeared on her 1994 album, "The Best…So Far".

Nightwing (film) | Nightwing is a 1979 American horror film directed by Arthur Hiller.  The screenplay by Martin Cruz Smith, Steve Shagan, and Bud Shrake is based on the 1977 novel of the same title by Smith.  Its tagline is "Day belongs to man, but night is theirs!"  It was one of many "Jaws" rip-offs that were popular in the late 1970s and early 1980s, including "" (1977), "Tentacles" (1977), "The Pack" (1977), "Piranha" (1978), "Alligator" (1980) and "Great White" (1980).  It also was Hiller's only horror film.

Bernard Hiller | Bernard Hiller, aka "Bernardo" born April 18, 1962 is an Actor, Singer, Dancer, Hollywood acting teacher, Producer, Writer, and Success coach.  Hiller was a Broadway performer in many musicals including "West Side Story" and Fiddler on the Roof".  He appeared in several TV shows and films such as Jean Paul in Raising Helen, and Max Lansky in Lansky.  He also appeared in over 200 TV commercials.
response.summary_2: Rip Taylor. Rip Taylor is an American actor/comedian known for his exuberant, flamboyant personality and his habit of showering himself (and others) with confetti.
